questions.question: What is the correct order of JVM execution stages?
options: JIT Compiler → Class Loader → Bytecode Verifier → Interpreter
Class Loader → Bytecode Verifier → Interpreter/JIT Compiler
Bytecode Verifier → Class Loader → JIT Compiler → Interpreter
Interpreter → Class Loader → JIT Compiler → Bytecode Verifier
questions.explanation: The JVM first loads classes (Class Loader), verifies bytecode safety (Bytecode Verifier), then executes via the Interpreter and JIT Compiler for hot paths.

questions.question: Which statement about JRE is correct as of Java 21?
options: JRE must be installed separately from the JDK
JRE is included in the JDK; standalone JRE was deprecated since Java 11
JRE has been completely removed and no longer exists
JRE is required for development but not for running applications
questions.explanation: The standalone JRE distribution was deprecated in Java 11. The JDK includes the runtime, so only the JDK needs to be installed.

questions.question: Which JDK tool creates custom minimal runtime images?
options: jpackage
jlink
jar
jconsole
questions.explanation: jlink creates custom, minimal JRE images containing only the modules your application needs, reducing distribution size.

questions.question: What does the single-file source launch feature (java MyApp.java) require?
options: Java 8+ and a module-info.java file
Java 11+ and the source must be a single file
Java 17+ and the --enable-preview flag
Java 9+ and a configured classpath
questions.explanation: Single-file source launch was introduced in Java 11. It compiles and runs a single .java file without an explicit javac step. It cannot reference other source files.

questions.question: What is the primary advantage of GraalVM's native-image over HotSpot JIT?
options: Higher peak throughput for long-running applications
Better garbage collection performance
Faster startup time and lower memory footprint
Built-in support for JPMS modules
questions.explanation: GraalVM native-image performs ahead-of-time (AOT) compilation to produce standalone binaries with near-instant startup and lower memory usage, ideal for CLI tools and serverless functions.
